keyEvents.event: Founding of the Kingdom by Ibn Saud
keyEvents.impact: Al Saud dynasty unified the peninsula in pact with Wahhabi clerics; religion underwrites royal legitimacy
Transformed a desert kingdom into the world's pivotal petroleum power and rentier state
Founded the oil-for-security bargain with the United States that anchors Saudi defense
Shocked the monarchy into empowering conservative clergy and exporting Wahhabism abroad

keyEvents.event: Gulf War basing of US troops
keyEvents.impact: Deepened reliance on American protection but fueled jihadist backlash against the dynasty

keyEvents.event: Intervention in Yemen against the Houthis
keyEvents.impact: Costly proxy quagmire with Iran; exposed limits of Saudi military power and oil infrastructure to attack

keyEvents.event: Rise of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman and Vision 2030
keyEvents.impact: Centralized power, curbed clergy, and launched sweeping economic diversification away from oil

history.narrative: Custodian of Islam's holiest sites and the swing producer of world oil, ruling through a bargain of petro-wealth, religious legitimacy, and American protection. Avoids direct wars but wages influence campaigns and proxy conflicts against Iran, while hedging between Washington, Beijing, and Moscow as it races to diversify its economy before the oil era ends.

redLines.description: Iranian regional dominance or military presence on the Arabian Peninsula
Attacks on Saudi oil infrastructure or closure of Gulf export routes
An Iranian nuclear weapon would trigger Saudi pursuit of matching capability

internalDivisions.name: Wahhabi establishment vs. top-down social liberalization
Shia minority in oil-rich Eastern Province
Royal family factions sidelined by MBS power consolidation

goals.description: Counter Iranian influence and proxy networks across the region
Diversify the economy away from oil under Vision 2030
Lead the Arab and Islamic worlds as custodian of the holy cities
Secure the Yemen border and end the Houthi missile threat
